I have made various clean up, changed some names, applied clang-format, removed a file and what not. The git log should give an overview. Various thoughts:

  1. We should IMHO not use __OCPN__ANDROID__ in new code. The __prefix is reserved for compilers and frameworks, and clang defines __ANDROID__ anyway. Let's use that in new code.
  2. commdriverN0183Serial.h: I think the interface is needlessly complicated, it should be possible to relegate commDriverN0183SerialThread and hence also n0183_atomic_queue to the cpp file, leaving just a commDriverN0183Serialin the header. After all, all there is is a pointer reference.
  3. Both N0183Serial.cpp and N2KSerial.cpp includes chart1.h and gFrame. I assume this is a transition state, this cannot be when we are done. On a sidenote, low-level code which includes the overall chart1.h is IMHO a major pain causing the "everything depends on everything" mess we have :(
  4. We have different naming conventions for identifiers and filenames causing inconsistencies . We cannot really discuss who or what is right , but it would be nice if could agree to use some convention out there. I will try to find something.
  5. The EVT_FOO event was a deliberate demo name. Actually, I'm a bit worried about the wxDEFINE_EVENT macro: since it is a macro I presume the namespace is global. However, it is possible to use the same event name is different parts of the code, and what that means is unclear. Ergo: we should some naming convention to ensure event names are unique. Including the class name would probably work (?)
Fixed 2., low hanging fruit.

1. OK
  2. Agree. n1083 drivers are incomplete, untested. Just there to exercise the class model. Will do. Done this way in the commDriverN2KSerial.cpp
  3. Yes, just there for initial component testing. Of course it badly breaks the module isolation logic. Goes away soon.
  4. OK.
  5. Are you suggesting that one event type would work for all n2k listeners/notifiers, for example? I wondered about that.
BTW, I appreciate your cleanup. Much easier to catch this stuff early in the cycle.

1. We basically use events in snippets like wxDEFINE_EVENT(FOO) and Bind(FOO ...). Each such "pair" so to speak needs a unique name of the event, otherwise things gets messed up in the observable core. In other words, never reuse an event name...
1. Done, look in master. More clean up in the cpp file possible,. though
1. Proposal for naming convention?

Not yet... But I guess that if the class name where the event is defined is included in the event name we should be fine. There might of course be several events in s single class, but this should be easier to handle.
As you can see, I used a more "functionally defined" name, e.g. EVT_N2K_129029

Actually, this might become a problem: If several classes, perhaps also plugins, listens to the same message they will end up using the same event name which possibly might be problem. To be honest, I'm not sure. Sigh...
We will find out when I start adapting Dashboard to the new model. Not real soon, though.
we (I) should read code and make simple test cases using opencpn-cmd framework

1. Naming conventions: We already use the google style for formatting . Why not use their naming style instead? https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Naming Thoughts?
    .
That is "why not use their naming style as well?" Damned language, this Englisch

I have no disagreement with Google naming style. It is very comprehensive. I fear it will take some iterations to make all this come naturally to me, so do not expect instant results. OTOH, I welcome corrections as you encounter really bad style examples in my work.

Working with the app_msg thing has clarified some things for me. One is that is becomes so much simpler if the the source part declares the shared_ptr instead of as now when it is declared inside the observed machinery. Practically, this would mean that notify should become notify(std::shared_ptr<NavMsg>)instead of notify(const NavMsg&) as of today. This also means that the source side needs to create the shared_ptr before sending it.

To me, it doesn't look that bad. It's two extra lines when sending a message. It also creates some symmetry where the Sink get the same pointer as provided by the Source.

Have you any strong opinions about this?

OTOH, I welcome corrections as you encounter really bad style examples in my work.

So do I. It is some uphill battle also for me. But, the sooner the better, before the amount of code explodes.

Tutorial: test/tests.cpp which has:

class SillyListener: public DriverListener {
public:
  /** Handle a received message. */
  virtual void notify(std::unique_ptr<const NavMsg> message)  {
    s_result2 = NavAddr::BusToString(message->bus);

    auto base_ptr = message.get();
    auto n2k_msg = dynamic_cast<const Nmea2000Msg*>(base_ptr);
    s_result3 = n2k_msg->id.to_string();

    stringstream ss;
    std::for_each(n2k_msg->payload.begin(), n2k_msg->payload.end(),
                  [&ss](unsigned char c) {ss << static_cast<char>(c); });
    s_result = ss.str();
  }

  /** Handle driver status change. */
  virtual void notify(const AbstractCommDriver& driver) {}
};

Fro the drivers perspective, this could be NavMsgBus (former Transport) or just anything. In the test it's used like this:

 auto indriver = std::make_shared<FileCommDriver>("/tmp/output.txt",
                                                   "/tmp/output.txt");
  auto listener = make_shared<SillyListener>();
  indriver->SetListener(listener);
  indriver->Activate();

which creates a FileCommdriver which writes to the SillyListener. In the "real" scenario, the driver will typically have the NavMsgBus there, But then again, it doesn't care, it writes whatever upper layer thinks it shoulkd write to.
OK, I get it. Another layer of abstraction upstream.

It's a known fact that all CS problems can be solved by yet another level of indirection ;)

In this case it's about layer isolation. We somehow need to handle for example NavMsgBus to the driver so it knows where it should send data. But we cannot let it know anything about NavMsgBus, since it is in a layer above. So, we turn it inside-out and let the NavMsgBus look at drivers and see how it must behave to be able to receive messages.

Also, as you can see this makes it dead simple to test. And that's actually a big deal IMHO.

void CommDriverRegistry::CloseAllDrivers() {
  for (auto& it : drivers)
